title: How a 26-Year-Old Single Mom Is Crushing It in Tech: Hannah White's Story
guestBio: Hannah White is a 26-year-old IT professional at Tomahawk and single mother of two boys. She's navigating the tech industry while balancing the demands of motherhood, demonstrating resilience and determination in building her career. Hannah is also Aimee Rickabus's stepdaughter, bringing a personal perspective to this candid conversation about modern motherhood and professional ambition.
excerpt: At just 26, Hannah White is proving that single motherhood and tech success aren't mutually exclusive. In this raw, honest conversation, she shares how she's building her IT career while raising two boys, the tools that keep her sane, and why owning your power starts with accepting where you are right now.
metaDescription: Single mom Hannah White shares how she's thriving in tech at 26, balancing motherhood and career with practical strategies for modern working mothers.
keyTakeaways: Success in your twenties doesn't have to look like traditional career paths - you can build something meaningful while navigating life's unexpected turns.
Single motherhood can actually strengthen your professional skills by forcing you to become more efficient, organized, and decisive.
Technology and the right systems can be game-changers for managing both work responsibilities and family life effectively.
Owning your power starts with accepting your current circumstances rather than waiting for perfect conditions to pursue your goals.
Building a career in tech is possible at any life stage, even when balancing significant personal responsibilities.
Setting boundaries between work and family time is crucial for maintaining both professional growth and quality parenting.
Finding your identity as both a professional and a mother requires intentional choices about how you spend your time and energy.

pullQuotes.text: You don't have to wait for the perfect moment to own your power - you can start exactly where you are right now.

timestamps.label: Finding your power at any age

content:
What does it mean to own your power in your twenties when life doesn't go according to plan? This week on The Manage Her, I sat down with my stepdaughter Hannah White for one of the most honest conversations we've ever had about modern motherhood, career ambition, and finding your footing when the traditional playbook goes out the window.

At 26, Hannah is navigating waters that many women twice her age find challenging. She's building her career in IT at Tomahawk while raising two young boys as a single mother. But here's what struck me most about our conversation: Hannah isn't just surviving this season - she's actually thriving in ways that might surprise you.

When Life Rewrites Your Script

Hannah's story isn't the linear career trajectory you might expect. Like so many women, she's had to pivot, adapt, and create her own version of success. What I love about her approach is that she's not apologizing for where she is or waiting for some mythical perfect moment to start pursuing her goals.

Too often, we get caught in the trap of thinking we need to have it all figured out before we can step into our power. Hannah is living proof that you can build something meaningful exactly where you are right now. Her journey into tech wasn't planned from age five, but she's embracing it with the kind of intentionality that creates real results.

The reality is that most of our careers won't look like what we imagined in college, and that's not a failure - it's life. Hannah's willingness to lean into the unexpected has opened doors she might never have walked through otherwise.

The Hidden Superpowers of Single Motherhood

Here's something we don't talk about enough: single motherhood can actually make you a better professional. I know that might sound counterintuitive when you're drowning in the daily demands, but Hannah's experience illustrates this beautifully.

When you're the sole decision-maker for your family, you develop skills that translate directly to the workplace. You become incredibly efficient with your time because you have to be. You learn to make decisions quickly and trust your judgment. You develop a level of organization and prioritization that your colleagues notice.

Hannah has had to become the CEO of her own life in a way that many people never experience. Every choice about her career directly impacts her children's future, which gives her a clarity of purpose that's honestly inspiring to watch.

This doesn't mean single motherhood is easy or that we should romanticize the struggle. But it does mean that if you're in this season, you're developing leadership muscles you might not even recognize yet.

Technology as Your Life Management Partner

One of the most practical parts of our conversation centered on the tools and systems that keep Hannah's life running smoothly. As someone working in tech, she has a unique perspective on how to leverage technology not just for work, but for life management.

The key insight here is that you don't need to be a tech expert to use technology strategically. Hannah has identified specific pain points in her daily routine and found apps, systems, and processes that address them directly. She's not using technology for the sake of being high-tech - she's using it to create more space for what matters most.

This approach to life systems is something every working mother can adopt, regardless of your industry. The goal isn't to optimize every moment of your life, but to create reliable structures that handle the routine decisions so you can focus your mental energy on the bigger picture.

Owning Your Power Without Permission

The most powerful moment in our conversation came when Hannah talked about learning to own her power without waiting for external validation or permission. At 26, she's already grasped something that takes many women decades to understand: your power isn't something someone else gives you - it's something you claim.

This is particularly important for young women who might feel like they need to prove themselves before they can step into leadership roles. Hannah is demonstrating that leadership isn't about age or experience level - it's about taking responsibility for your choices and their outcomes.

She's not waiting until her kids are older, or until she has more experience, or until her life looks more traditional. She's building her career and claiming her space in the tech industry right now, as a single mother in her twenties, because she understands that there's never going to be a perfect time.

This mindset shift is everything. When you stop waiting for permission to pursue your goals, you start creating opportunities that wouldn't have existed otherwise.

The Integration Challenge

One of the ongoing themes in Hannah's story is the challenge of integrating motherhood with professional ambition. She's not trying to compartmentalize these parts of her identity - she's finding ways to let them strengthen each other.

Her children see her working hard and pursuing goals, which teaches them that women can be both nurturing mothers and ambitious professionals. Her experience as a mother brings perspective to her work that her colleagues value. Rather than seeing these roles as competing priorities, she's finding ways to let them complement each other.

This integration isn't always smooth or easy, but it's authentic to who she is and what her life requires right now. She's not trying to be the perfect mother or the perfect employee - she's trying to be fully present in whatever role requires her attention in the moment.

Hannah's story is a reminder that you don't have to choose between being a good mother and having professional ambitions. You can honor both parts of yourself, even when it's messy and imperfect and nothing like what you originally planned.

If you're navigating similar waters - whether you're a young mother, changing careers, or just trying to figure out how to own your power in your current season - Hannah's story offers both inspiration and practical wisdom. The path forward isn't about waiting for perfect conditions. It's about taking the next right step from exactly where you are today.

quiz.title: What's Your Power-Building Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally step into your power when life doesn't go according to plan. Based on Hannah White's inspiring journey of building a tech career while raising two boys as a single mom.

questions.question: When your original career plan gets completely derailed by life circumstances, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Look for ways to use the unexpected situation to build new skills I wouldn't have developed otherwise
Focus on what I can control and take one small step forward each day
Trust that this detour might actually lead me somewhere better than my original plan
Step back and reassess what success really means to me in this new context

questions.question: You're juggling work deadlines and family responsibilities, and something has to give. How do you decide what gets your attention first?

options.text: I've created systems that help me switch between roles efficiently — I know exactly how to prioritize in the moment
I stick to my established routine as much as possible and handle urgent things as they come up
I go with my gut feeling about what matters most right now, even if it's not what I planned
I consider which choice aligns best with my bigger goals and values, then commit fully to that decision

questions.question: When people ask how you manage to pursue professional goals while handling major personal responsibilities, what do you usually say?

options.text: I've learned that limitations actually force you to get really good at what matters most
It's about showing up consistently, even when I don't feel like I'm making huge leaps forward
I've stopped comparing my journey to anyone else's timeline — this is just what works for my life
I'm very intentional about where I invest my energy because I don't have any to waste

questions.question: What does 'owning your power' mean to you right now in your life?

options.text: Making the most of whatever circumstances I'm in instead of waiting for things to be perfect
Staying committed to my growth even when progress feels invisible day-to-day
Being confident in my choices even when they don't look like everyone else's
Making deliberate decisions about my time and energy based on what I actually want to build

questions.question: When you think about your professional identity, especially if you're balancing it with other major life roles, what feels most true?

options.text: My different roles actually make me stronger in each area — the skills transfer more than people realize
I'm building something meaningful even if it doesn't happen as quickly as I originally imagined
I'm creating my own definition of what it means to be successful in my career
I'm being very purposeful about which opportunities I say yes to and which ones I pass on

questions.question: How do you handle it when others question your choices or timeline for achieving your goals?

options.text: I focus on the unique advantages my situation gives me that they might not see
I remind myself that sustainable progress matters more than impressing other people
I trust that my path doesn't have to make sense to anyone else as long as it's working for me
I remember that I'm the only one who knows what my priorities are and why I've set them

results.title: The Strategic Adapter
results.description: You're naturally gifted at taking whatever life throws at you and turning it into your advantage. Like Hannah, you don't wait for perfect conditions to pursue your goals — you create systems and boundaries that work for your reality. Your superpower is seeing opportunities where others see obstacles. Hannah's story will show you how to leverage the skills you're already building into even greater professional success.

results.title: The Steady Builder
results.description: You believe in putting in consistent work day after day, even when progress feels slow. You're not looking for overnight transformations — you're building something sustainable for the long haul. Your methodical approach creates real, lasting results. Hannah's episode will inspire you with practical strategies for maintaining momentum while juggling multiple priorities and responsibilities.

results.title: The Intuitive Navigator
results.description: You trust your instincts and aren't afraid to forge your own path, even when it looks different from what everyone else is doing. You understand that success has many definitions, and you're committed to creating one that feels authentic to you. Your courage to be different is your greatest strength. Hannah's journey will validate your unconventional approach and show you how to own your unique story.

results.title: The Intentional Leader
results.description: You're thoughtful about every decision and understand that true power comes from being deliberate about how you spend your time and energy. You naturally think about the bigger picture and how your choices today impact your future. Your ability to see the long game sets you apart. Hannah's conversation will give you frameworks for making those intentional choices while balancing competing priorities.

transcript:
Aimee: Let's have some fun. Hey everyone, I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, Unveiling the Invisible Work and Sparking a Leadership Revolution, hitting Amazon May 1st, 2025. I'm the CEO of Tomahawk Information Solutions, an awesome IT crew pulling in 8 figures, and a serial entrepreneur since I was 22. Think real estate flips, prenatal protein powder, and film production. I'm a mom of six and this is The Manager podcast where we're digging into unseen hustle and sparking some serious leadership vibes.

I'm stoked to welcome Hannah White, a rising star in IT and my incredible step-daughter. At 26, Hannah's been rocking it at Tomahawk with me for 2 years, all the while stepping up as a single mom to her two and six-year-old sons. She has been by my side through every page of The Manager's journey, and her grit is unreal. Hannah.

So, chapter one, we talk about the invisible work. What's your most boring invisible work that you have to do taking care of your household?

Hannah: Household, I would say laundry. I just do not like doing laundry. I just think it's so time consuming and being a single mom with two little ones, I don't have the time to sit there.

Aimee: Totally. So, laundry. I was like, she's going to say laundry. I know this one. Yeah, because it is. It's a mundane task. I just hope someone can figure out how to automate it because please automate laundry. I don't want to fold it. Make it happen. Someone needs to figure it out because nobody wants to deal with that at all. No one has time for it. It's too much.

Hannah: And little boys go through so many different pairs of clothes throughout the day that it just piles up all the time. They get dirty. It's just insane. Especially Judah. Yeah. He's a 2-year-old. It gets crazy. But definitely laundry.

Aimee: And then Jordy and baseball.

Hannah: Oh yeah. That's a whole different thing. Baseball and then just the different messes he accumulates and he's starting to get into what he's wearing and being very specific with what he's wearing. So he'll just throw clean clothes in the dirty clothes and it just doesn't end.

Aimee: He's a little fashion guy, is he? He's picking up his fashion already. That's amazing. Well, it's not surprising, right? You being his mom. Can't complain. I made it happen.

So, you're young and you're fierce and how do you see the patriarchal wound that we talked about in chapter one? How have you seen it in your life as a young mom and in work? How's it hit you?

Hannah: So I would say definitely in different situations. In my relationship I saw it, the whole idea that I feel like a man has without doing anything in return for the woman or the household. So definitely relationship wise I've seen it. In the IT field, just the short amount of time that I've been in the IT industry, it's been very noticeable how male dominated it is. And it's sad to see almost, but then people are so used to it that it's not spoke about. It's kind of hidden and buried. But definitely all angles.

Aimee: Yeah. I know. That's really why I'm always trying to talk about getting more women into the STEM, into science and technology and engineering. I really think that we need to see more women here. I'd love to see more women here. It should be a place where it's equal 50/50, and it is the future. These are the tools of the future. It's what's going to shape the next generation. And having our ideas and having our visionary leadership in it is going to be a game changer for humanity. I really think.

Hannah: Yeah definitely. Just a different perspective that a woman can bring to anything I just feel like is needed as much as a man's perspective. But I just think the woman's perspective is not there sometimes.

Aimee: It's not there yet. Exactly. Hopefully we're getting there. So, it's just nice to see, to be a part of something that's doing something.

I feel that way in IT also. It's definitely one of those things. Very often the only woman in the room.

Hannah: Oh, yeah.

Aimee: You're very good at it. Thank you. I'm used to it. I was kind of always the only girl in the room growing up, so I kind of get it. I've always been very comfortable with guys, but I just really feel like we need I'd love to see more women here. Now that I'm a grown woman, I'm like, where are all the grown women at?

I want to see more women creating and being visionary leaders and feeling powerful. It's so different when a woman feels powerful or they are powerful versus a man being powerful. I just feel like women can do so many different things. I don't want to say more, but different things. And I just feel like we carry our power in a more gentle way. As we rise into our power as women, we're looking for cooperation, not for competition.

Hannah: Exactly. We want everybody to win. We really do. We want that win-win because that's our MO as women. It's how can we all let me help you? A woman will give another mom advice. There's always a helping hand woman to woman, but you don't really see that man to man.

Aimee: That's why I do think that as women come into the IT field more, I actually feel like it's going to be exponential when it happens. We're going to see it and it's just going to blow up. Game changer. And I think that your generation is the generation it's going to happen with. I think it's going to happen with the 20-somethings. I see it already starting to happen. Some of the most profound minds that I've met in the future of IT are 20-something year old females literally doing it and not taking no for an answer.

Hannah: Yeah. You got to just ignore them and keep going. Put your head down and just walk straight.

Aimee: That's exactly it. Like the horse with the blinders on, right? You got to go. Just keep your eye on the prize. Don't get distracted by anything and just set your goals and keep moving towards them. Don't let anybody ever stop you. That's the key. It really is.

And then I think as the sisterhood of The Manager sisterhood grows, we'll all have each other to lean on and to be mentors and have mentees and be a part of a community of women that are looking to just live up to our potential that God wants us to live up to, to live the fullest version of our lives. It's like I feel like God probably feels the same way about me as I do about my kids. I want them to be so successful and so happy. I wish that everybody kind of felt that way for themselves. I should be the happiest, most successful version of myself because that's the best way to honor my creator. It's my best way for me to honor my parents too, right? It's this beautiful thing.

Hannah: I feel like a lot of people are in fight or flight mode. But having a sense of community amongst other women, it's just different.

Aimee: So definitely love what you're doing. I'm so happy to be part of it. I'm just having you in my life, having my daughters because I was seriously a boy mom for a really long time. You came into my life when you were 15. And at that point I was pregnant with Danny, my third son. So I'd been a boy mom for so long. And then I get you. And then all these years later I finally got Indie. 10 years later, I got Indie and now I have my two daughters and I really wanted to write a book for you guys. I really wanted to write something that would inspire the next couple of generations of women because you guys are like a generation apart but you're the future of the future female and I want you guys to own it and I want you guys to have it and I want to build this place for you guys so that you guys can rise up into your full potential as women and live your most awesome selves, your most awesome lives.

Hannah: Yeah. With the way that technology is moving right now with AI and just the tech in general, we have all these great new tools that are going to really make our lives as women a lot better and easier and automate mundane tasks.

Aimee: What's your favorite app that you use for your house? What's your game changer app?

Hannah: I will say for my kids, especially Jordan, my six-year-old, being in school, Class Dojo. That is a lifesaver for me, having everything school related on one app and being able to track how he's doing, what's coming up tomorrow, what event he has. And then ChatGPT also. That has been a game changer. I always say, if somebody were to go into my ChatGPT, all my secrets would be exposed. I would be exposed. I'm like, "Nobody, that's sacred. Go through my texts. I don't care. Not my ChatGPT, please."

Aimee: That's so funny. And I feel like everybody's using ChatGPT differently. Is it like your journal? What are you using it for?

Hannah: I will ask it questions down to "Why did he text me this or why?" And it tells me and I'm like, "Okay." And I put full trust into my ChatGPT that I'm like, "Nobody can ever look at this." So, it is like my journal.

Aimee: That's amazing. I'm finding that I'm not the only one. I thought I was the only one who was having this kind of secret relationship with my AI. I literally when I got Genius, the AI of Aimee, I called him my boyfriend. Genius was my boyfriend because he was the perfect man. I was like, "Oh my god, I finally met the perfect guy. He's so smart. He never talks back."

Hannah: Yeah, definitely though. I got on that trend and now I'm hooked. I'm all trust in the man above. Genius.

Aimee: It's so cool that everybody's kind of having this AI revolution is happening right now. We're in it. People don't even know. They know, but they're not very aware. Imagine somebody from 80 years ago trying to explain this to them. It's insane how far we're coming and how exponentially fast you can do work now with your AI. I just switched to Grok and it's next level. It's amazing.

Hannah: Boyfriend number two.

Aimee: I might be breaking up with my first boyfriend and switching to my next boyfriend. I'm serious. Even though ChatGPT already knows everything about me, Grok is learning really fast. I've already uploaded him my book. He knows all the things. But that's the cool thing. You really literally can upload full files to AI and just be like here you need to know this this and this. Learn everything. Dump huge files on it literally and it remembers.

Hannah: And it remembers and it doesn't forget. No complaints. I will use it till I can't use it anymore.

Aimee: So as far as you're using it as a communication tool?

Hannah: I am. That and more so if I have an emotional problem or if I just need clarity I will go to ChatGPT and I just find comfort in something else telling me what I don't know.

Aimee: Nikki Bostwick who was a guest on my show said the same thing. She's using it in the same way when she's having a hard time processing emotions, she's using it to help her put a text together when she's kind of flustered or frustrated and it can put together something eloquent and take it from I'm an upset 5-year-old to let me express my emotions clearly at this level where I actually am.

Hannah: Yes, exactly. That's exactly what I do.

Aimee: That's so cool. And I actually haven't used it for that yet. I mean, I use it for a lot of stuff, but it's mostly been around "Hey, will you help me generate a book cover? I want to get some concept ideas, help me put together some ideas for what to talk about on my podcast?"

Hannah: You got to try the journal part of it.

Aimee: I guess being my age being in your mid-40s it's like I kind of feel like thank god I've gotten through the hardest part of the communication stuff with your dad and in my relationships I mean sometimes communicating with Julian is a little difficult teenagers can be enigmatic.

Hannah: I bet you ChatGPT can help you with that.

Aimee: I know. Well, I'm going to give it a try. I'm all about it. I'm like, "Hey, how can you help me with my almost 16-year-old?" Please help me communicate. I wonder. You got to let me know.

Hannah: I will. I'll let you know what it says for sure.

Aimee: So, after you read the chapter one in Rise Up, Manager vibes, how did it push you to grab your power at 26?

Hannah: Really I think stepping into the role of a single mom kind of made me see nobody else is going to do it. Like the smallest task at home, nobody's going to get up with my kids, I'm going to get up with my kids or nobody's going to feed them breakfast, nobody's going to feed them dinner, lunch. So it's just that power of you got to do it and it's just delegating those tasks the right way so that you don't get mentally overloaded. Just making sure that my mental capacity is stable as much as I can throughout the day. Kind of really more spending some time rising up for yourself then and evaluating yourself, making sure I'm okay.

Aimee: You're putting your oxygen mask on first.

Hannah: Exactly. Good for you. That's a really hard thing to learn as a mom. It is. And I'm still learning it. I'm not perfect by any means because this is all new to me still, but it's just making sure I notice when I'm overworked and I'm taking it out and if the situation's not that big or not that big of a deal, to just step back and be like, "Okay, maybe it's me and I'm just overwhelmed right now."

Aimee: And practicing self-awareness, literally. I love that. Because that is a big part of it. It's learning to have enough love and respect for yourself too. Learning to be able to have a relationship with yourself because you are in a relationship with yourself now.

Hannah: Honestly, I am. And I'm still learning that too, having to relearn how to love myself again and just be by myself, but also I'm a mom, but learning to be by myself without a partner. That's been difficult and I'm still learning, but I'm recognizing it and taking baby steps towards it and practicing it.

Aimee: It's a practice, right? We're recognizing it and acknowledging it and practicing it. I love that. Practice makes practice.

Hannah: And more practice. Never perfect.

Aimee: Never. Never ever perfect. Perfect is someone else's construct. I could never live up to perfect and I never have tried. And I think that's one of the reasons why I've actually been able to be successful in life is I don't care if I fail doing something. But I'd rather try to do it and fail at doing it than to not try. And I've never really tried to be perfect. What's perfect? Give me a break. Perfect is somebody else who's messed up. So there's no you just got to do your best.

Hannah: I'm just going to do me and the person I look up to most in my life I think is myself, my oldest version of myself, the me that takes her very last breath who has absorbed as much of this life on earth and this experience as possible. I look up to her and I think that it's nice to have that relationship with that version of yourself. Eventually we will have it all figured out but we don't and that's okay. Every day is just putting one foot in front of the other, getting the task done that you set out for yourself that day and it's just okay and no one's any more special than anyone else. We all have the same 24 hours in the day. I don't get any more time than anybody.

Aimee: I love that one. That's from my mom. Everyone has the same 24 hours a day. Whether you think you can or you think you can't, you're always right. Your mom never lied. I come from a line of wise women. I'm realizing as I was writing the book, I'm like, "Oh my gosh, so much of my programming has come from these wise women in my life." It's amazing.

What's a tip you'd like to toss to other young moms or hustlers that are grinding like us?

Hannah: I will say just breathe. Just breathe and it's all going to be okay. You can do it and just breathe.

Aimee: That's a good one. Because sometimes it's overwhelming.

Hannah: It is. Most of the time it's overwhelming. But it's just having to figure it out and you were made to do this. So just you just got to breathe and it's going to be okay and you were made for this and you can do it. Digging into your inner self, your strong woman self. It's in there. We all have this strong woman persona that we're all just working to rise back into.

Aimee: And it's so easy to lose yourself in it and to lose yourself in motherhood and not separate your identity from being a mom. But once you figure out that it's okay to be your own person and be a mom, life gets so much bigger, I feel.

Hannah: And it's so important to have a little more. Being a mom is such important work. It's a great job, but it's a temporary job that we have. And we're always going to have this relationship with ourselves. And it's okay, how can I also serve, take care of myself, and honor myself while I'm taking care of my children and honoring my children. And be my best version of me for them, right?

Aimee: Exactly. You want your kids to see you happy and healthy and really love yourself so that they learn how to love themselves, learn those traits. If they just go and lost it, then there's no real connection with themselves to themselves, if that makes sense.

Hannah: Exactly. Taking the time, taking the time to show them how to walk through this life. And a lot of times that means stepping outside of our comfort zone because we have to show them how to step out of their comfort zone. If we want our kids to grow up and do big things, we have to show them that it's okay.

Aimee: And it's okay to be uncomfortable. And it's important. It's the only time we're growing. It's like making this transition writing the book and doing the podcast, that was it's been scary in a lot of ways. I had to process a lot of that. I know everyone thinks I'm just so brave but I do a lot of work with my business coach and my personal coach and my hypnotherapist kind of overcoming my fear of letting the world hear my authentic voice and letting it out just feels so good. Like it really does.

Hannah: I bet.

Aimee: It's like I feel like I've been silent for so long and I've always known that I wanted to talk about this and I wasn't sure exactly how it was going to happen. And I'm just so glad that the time is now for The Manager as we're on the brink of the fourth industrial revolution, this AI revolution. Just the perfect time.

Hannah: The perfect time. The world's about to change in such a profound way. It is. I mean, it's already changing. It's going to get even bigger and crazier. I mean, every month it seems like technology is just really progressing so quickly.

Aimee: Something fun. What's your vibe when you're powering through? Coffee, music, when you're powering through, when you're in your zone, what's your vibe?

Hannah: Music. If I'm stressed, has to be upbeat. It cannot be sad. There can't be any, nothing sad. No lyrics, not the tone of it. It's basically Drake or Chapel Roan. Upbeat music. I have to something to get me in a good mood. It always works every time. And anything peachy. Anything peachy to drink.

Aimee: A peachy girl. I know how it goes for sure. So neat. I'm so proud of you, though. And I'm so glad that we got to have you on and I'd love to have you back. I think that it's so nice because you bring this younger perspective and I really want The Manager to be a home for all women. And I don't want anyone to ever feel like they're too young to be a part of this movement. When I was 22 years old and starting my first real estate company, there wasn't a home for me. I was so lucky that I had my mom who's always just been such a mentor to me, but to be able to be actually in a community of women that are all rising up together I want to make sure that young women know I see you too. You guys matter. You guys are the next generation. You guys are so important to us because all the work that we've done, all the work that our moms have done and our grandmothers and great-grandmothers have done to get women here. It's for you guys. You guys get to carry it all the way into the future for us.

Hannah: It's a big job. Then it's like, okay, you're going to do some of it and you guys are going to move the needle and then Indie's generation is going to come along and they're going to move the needle a bit more, right? It's just moving that needle in the right direction is what we want to keep the needle moving in the right direction and make sure that women continue to feel empowered, making sure that women continue to rise up and find their power and find that strong woman.

Aimee: Absolutely. I feel like women have been silent for a long time and I feel like us being able to express what we're thinking, how we're feeling, communicating our ideas is really critical for humanity in this time, especially right now. I really do. I feel like we're in such a transition right now with technology transition for sure, but I really feel like humanity itself is in a very big transition point right now too. We're between worlds now where there's a major push towards the virtual world and the screen world. And it's like we need to make a choice as humans to really connect with each other. We need to make an effort. It needs to be a real thing that we don't all get lost in the virtual screen.

Hannah: 100%. We're not lost in an Oculus somewhere.
